1:
The chapter opens on a chronological knot that the witnesses attack from several directions at once, and on the very name of the king who is its subject. The verse dates Azariah's accession to "the twenty-seventh year of Jeroboam," yet Amaziah his father, on the plain figures of the previous chapters, lived only to about Jeroboam's fifteenth year. The reformed John Gill lays out the whole array of solutions without committing to one: some insist on a corruption of numbers, twenty-seven for seventeen; Ben Gersom and Abarbanel suppose the twenty-seven years are not past but reckon forward to the extinction of Jehu's house, counting Jeroboam's twenty-six years and Zachariah's six months as twenty-seven imperfect years; others posit that Jeroboam reigned eleven or twelve years alongside his father, so that the count may be taken from different starting points; and still others reckon Azariah's reign variously, either from the time Amaziah fled to Lachish or from his death.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott cuts the knot with a single stroke: "the twenty-seventh year" is simply an error of transcription for the fifteenth, the confusion of the Hebrew numeral letters being easy, and the true date fixed by the figures of 2 Kings 14:2 and following — Amaziah reigned twenty-nine years, fourteen concurrently with Joash and fifteen with Jeroboam, so that Uzziah succeeded in Jeroboam's fifteenth year. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, surveying the same tangle, throws up his hands: he records Lightfoot's attempt to reconcile it by supposing Uzziah only four years old at his father's death, the throne empty eleven years and the government held by protectors during his minority, but concludes that learned men are not agreed and that "there is probably some mistake in the numbers," quoting the tag that it is not for him to settle so great a dispute.

Beneath the chronology lies a quieter textual point. Clarke, following Kennicott, notes that the king's name is corrupted into four different forms in this one chapter, and that the oldest Hebrew manuscript reads truly "Uzziah" — the form the printed text itself uses at v.32 and v.34, supported by the Syriac and Arabic and by the "Ozias" of the genealogy in Matthew. Ellicott adds a startling external witness: an Azriyahu, king of Judah, appears in two fragmentary inscriptions of Tiglath-Pileser, connected with a revolt of nineteen districts of Hamath — though he grants this conflicts with the ordinary chronology that fixes Azariah's death about 758 B.C. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the closing phrase "to reign" as meaning that Azariah now began solely and fully to exercise the royal power. The witnesses do not so much divide as offer rival keys to a lock none can turn with full confidence; what they agree on is that the difficulty is real and the reign it introduces genuine.
2:
The regnal formula is bare — sixteen years old at his accession, fifty-two years in Jerusalem, his mother Jecholiah of the city — but the witnesses fill it out from the fuller record elsewhere. The reformed John Gill notes only that he began by the consent of the people and princes of Judah, a peaceable succession that the northern kingdom, as the chapter will show, could no longer manage. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch dwell on the strange silence of the formula: not a single special act is recorded of a fifty-two-year reign, although 2 Chronicles 26 tells of a kingdom raised to great earthly power and prosperity.

Keil and Delitzsch propose a reason for the omission. The historian passes over Uzziah's achievements, they suggest, precisely because they left no permanent mark on the kingdom of Judah — all the useful fruits of his long reign were undone again by the ungodly Ahaz his grandson. The evaluation itself is qualified in a way the fuller history explains: Uzziah "did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, as his father Amaziah had done," but as Amaziah proved unfaithful in his closing years, so Uzziah sought God only so long as Zechariah, the man experienced in divine visions, remained alive. While that seeking lasted, God gave success to his enterprises.

The witnesses reconstruct those enterprises from the Chronicler: successful wars against the Philistines and the Arabians, the walls of Jerusalem fortified with strong towers, watch-towers built in the desert, and cisterns dug for the protection of his herds. It is a portrait of vigour and competence, and the two witnesses agree that its very brilliance makes the leprosy of v.5 the more tragic — a mighty and prosperous king who, at the height of his power, would overreach into the priest's office and be struck down. The formula, then, is not empty but reticent; it records the length and the general fidelity of the reign, and leaves to the reader who consults the parallel the greatness that was and the pride that spoiled it. Keil and Delitzsch make the deeper point that Uzziah's godliness was tethered to a living prophet: while Zechariah lived, the king sought God, and the seeking prospered; the reign is thus a study in a piety that outlasts neither its counsellor nor its success, a warning folded quietly into a formula that seems to say nothing at all.
3:
"He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his father Amaziah had done" — and the witnesses probe just how far that commendation reaches. The reformed John Gill enters an immediate qualification: the king did right "at the beginning of his reign, and in an external way," a hint that both the timing and the depth of his obedience were limited. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the same point into a definition. The phrase, repeated word for word in Chronicles, means here as elsewhere that Azariah supported the legitimate worship and lent his countenance to no foreign cultus; it is a verdict on his religious policy, not a certificate of inward holiness.

That distinction lets Ellicott reconcile the historian with the Chronicler. When the chronicler adds that Uzziah "sought God in the days of Zechariah," and that "as long as he sought Jehovah, God made him to prosper," he does not contradict the general estimate here but simply supplies further information about the king's life and fortunes — the reservation "as long as" already implying that the seeking did not last. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills in what that prospering looked like from 2 Chron. 26:5: Uzziah fought the Philistines and broke down the walls of Gath, Jabneh, and Ashdod; prevailed over the Arabians and the Mehunims; received tribute from the Ammonites; extended his dominion to the border of Egypt; built towers in Jerusalem and in the desert and dug many wells; and maintained a strong, well-regulated army furnished with engines to shoot arrows and hurl great stones, so that his fame spread far abroad.

The witnesses converge on a single reading of the verse. The commendation is true but bounded: Uzziah was faithful to the LORD's worship and was rewarded with a prosperity the parallel history sets out in glowing detail, yet the "right" he did was an external conformity that endured only while a prophet stood at his side. Gill's terse "in an external way" and Ellicott's careful "religious policy, not inward holiness" say the same thing from different schools, and Clarke's catalogue of triumphs supplies the reward that made the later fall so steep. None of them contradicts the others; together they set a prosperous, orthodox, but shallow-rooted king before us, whose right-doing was real enough to be blessed and thin enough to be broken. The verse thus becomes a study in a goodness measured by its outward acts, and the reader is left to feel how much such a goodness needed the prophet whose death would loose the king to his ruinous pride.
4:
The single reservation on Uzziah's obedience is the one the book enters on nearly every good king of Judah: "Save that the high places were not removed: the people sacrificed and burnt incense still on the high places." Only one witness speaks here, and briefly, but the reformed John Gill draws the verse into the long pattern it belongs to. Uzziah did right, he observes, "excepting in that instance" — the high places — and the exception was no novelty peculiar to him but "the case of his father and other kings before him, and others afterwards, till Hezekiah came."

Gill's economy is itself instructive. He does not treat the surviving high places as a small thing casually left standing; he treats them as a chronic, inherited failure that ran unbroken through reign after reign of otherwise faithful kings. The good kings of Judah suppressed idolatry and upheld the temple worship, yet none of them, from Asa and Jehoshaphat down through Joash and Amaziah to Uzziah himself, mustered the resolve to abolish the local shrines where the people, even in the name of the LORD, offered sacrifice and incense outside the one sanctuary God had chosen. It was not until Hezekiah, and after him Josiah, that the axe was finally laid to them.

The verse thus measures both the reach and the limit of a reformation carried only so far. The people are named as the actors — "the people sacrificed and burnt incense still" — so that the fault is not the king's alone but a popular attachment the throne would not override. Uzziah tolerated what he might have removed, and in tolerating it left a standing temptation in the land, a habit of worship at the wrong places that no external orthodoxy could dislodge. Gill's brief note, set against the many verses that repeat this same "howbeit," lets the reader feel the weight of a good reign that stopped short: the high places were the one thing a whole succession of pious kings would not touch, and their persistence is the quiet flaw threaded through even the best chapters of Judah's history. The single dissenting clause, small as it looks, is the seed of much later trouble, for what a good king merely tolerates a bad king will exploit, and the shrines Uzziah spared will stand ready for the idolatry of his grandson.
5:
The one peculiar event of the reign is told with a kind of awe — "the LORD smote the king, so that he was a leper unto the day of his death" — and the witnesses gather to explain why. The reformed John Gill gives the reason in a line: the king intruded into the priest's office, going into the temple to burn incense on the altar. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke tells the whole scene from 2 Chron. 26:16: Uzziah's heart was lifted up, and he went in to burn incense, assuming to himself the high priest's function; Azariah the priest with fourscore others followed to stop him; and while they remonstrated the LORD struck him with leprosy that broke out on his forehead, so that they thrust him out and he himself hurried to be gone, feeling that God had smitten him. Clarke cannot resist appending Charles Wesley's stinging verses against those who, without lawful ordination, seize the priest's office — "the loathsome leprosy of pride" — and applies them to every man who through pride or gain intrudes into a ministry he was never called to.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds precision to every clause. The stroke fell towards the end of the reign; the word "smote" is the language of a plague or paralytic stroke. The "several house" he renders not a vague separate dwelling but a sickhouse — a royal residence outside Jerusalem set apart for such cases, as Lev. 13:46 and 2 Kings 7:3 prescribe — noting the Hebrew strictly means "house of freedom," because lepers were emancipated from all social relations and duties. Jotham being "over the house" he takes to mean not steward of the palace but dwelling in it in his father's place, and "judging the people of the land" to mean acting as his father's representative.

Here Ellicott turns the verse against a favourite harmonizing device. This passage, he insists, is strong evidence against the assumption of joint sovereignties of princes with their fathers, so often invoked to escape chronological difficulties in Hebrew history: Jotham is not co-regent but viceroy of Azariah until the latter dies, standing in for a father shut away by disease, not sharing a crown. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley simply points back to 2 Chron. 26:16 for the cause. The witnesses do not divide on the meaning of the plague — it is God's humbling of royal pride, the presumption of the temple answered by exclusion from the palace — but Ellicott's reading opens a quiet dispute with those interpreters, seen already at v.1, who resolve the chapter's numbers by multiplying co-regencies. The man who grasped at a priest's censer ends his days a leper in a house of freedom, shut out from temple and palace alike, while his son governs in his name.
6:
The closing-formula points the reader elsewhere for the fuller story — "the rest of the acts of Azariah, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?" — and the two witnesses here are chiefly concerned with where that fuller story may be found. The reformed John Gill notes that some of these acts are preserved in the canonical book of Chronicles, and adds a tradition that others were written by the prophet Isaiah, whose ministry overlapped the reign; the historian's cross-reference is thus not to a lost void but to records some of which survive to us.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott inventories what those acts were: Uzziah's wars with the Philistines and the Arabs, his improvements in the organisation of the army and the defences of the capital, his fondness for husbandry and cattle-breeding and his success in all these directions, and — not least — his intrusion into the sanctuary to offer incense at the golden altar, the very act that cost him his health. He directs the reader to 2 Chronicles 26 for the full account.

Brief as the formula is, the witnesses let it do a pointed work. The historian of Kings, who has told us almost nothing of Uzziah's greatness and only the fact of his leprosy, defers the record of a genuinely remarkable reign to other books, keeping his own narrative lean. What he will not omit is the leprosy; what he is content to leave to the chronicles is the glory. That proportion is itself a judgment: the achievements of a long and prosperous reign — the towers, the wells, the victories, the agriculture — are gathered into a single sentence of reference, while the stroke that humbled the king's pride is told at length. Gill and Ellicott do not divide; between them they show a king whose deeds filled the annals of two nations, and whose one act of presumption filled the memory of Scripture. The formula, far from being a mere editorial seam, quietly teaches the reader which acts of a king endure in the sight of God and which are left to the bookkeeping of men — and it is telling that the historian's chosen memorial of this great king is not his conquests but his fall.
7:
"So Azariah slept with his fathers; and they buried him with his fathers in the city of David: and Jotham his son reigned in his stead." The reformed John Gill marks the plain fact in a phrase — the king "died, when he had reigned fifty-two years" — the settled idiom closing the longest reign the book has yet recorded. But the burial notice carries a wrinkle the witnesses will not let pass. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that although the verse says Uzziah was buried "with his fathers in the city of David," the Chronicler completes the account: because he died of leprosy he was laid not in the royal family tomb itself but "in the burial-field of the kings," in a plot near them rather than among them.

The detail is small but eloquent. The leprosy that had shut Uzziah out of the temple and out of his own palace followed him even into the grave. In life the law of the leper had emancipated him from society and confined him to his sickhouse, as v.5 recorded; in death the same uncleanness kept his bones a little apart from the fathers with whom he was nonetheless numbered. He is buried "with his fathers" and yet not quite among them — honoured with the dignity of the royal cemetery, yet marked to the last by the stroke that had humbled his pride.

The verse also turns the page of the succession. Jotham, who had already been "over the house" and judging the people as his father's viceroy, now reigns in his own right, the transition made smooth by the years of regency the leprosy had forced. The witnesses do not divide; they simply let the closing formula gather up the reign — its length, its greatness passed over, its one great humbling — and hand the throne to a son who had already learned to govern in his father's shadow. So ends a reign of fifty-two years: a king carried to the tombs of David's line, buried with his fathers and yet set a pace apart, the memory of his presumption clinging to him past death, and a well-prepared heir stepping quietly into his place. The one great king of this chapter goes to his grave marked by the one great sin of his reign, and the crown passes, for once, without a drop of blood.
8:
With Zachariah the northern kingdom enters its death-throes, and the witnesses treat this first short reign as the doorway into a long anarchy. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry pauses at the threshold to survey the whole grim stretch that follows — some thirty-three years in which the crown passes by murder from hand to hand. The best days of Israel, he says, were while the government stayed in Jehu's family; now that those days are ended, the history represents the affairs of the kingdom "in the utmost confusion imaginable." He draws out its lessons: God had tried Israel with judgments and mercies and found them impenitent, and so brought on the miseries Moses had warned of in Lev. 26:21; God kept his promise to Jehu that his sons to the fourth generation should sit on the throne, and in Zachariah, the fourth, that word was exactly fulfilled; yet every one of these kings did evil, walking in the sins of Jeroboam, so that though at variance with one another they agreed to keep up the calves. Each usurper in turn — Shallum, Menahem, Pekah, and Hoshea — waded to the throne through the blood of his predecessor, and while the nation tore itself the kings of Assyria came and did as they pleased, for nothing makes a people so easy a prey as intestine broil.

Beneath Henry's overview the reformed John Gill and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch wrestle with a hard chronological gap. Zachariah began, the verse says, in the thirty-eighth year of Azariah; but if Azariah's accession fell in Jeroboam's twenty-seventh year and Jeroboam reigned forty-one years, Jeroboam's death fell about Azariah's fifteenth or sixteenth year, leaving a long vacancy before his son took the throne. Gill reckons an interregnum "of twenty-two years at least," owing to dissensions among the princes and a dislike of Zachariah. Keil and Delitzsch, following the chronologists since Usher, make it eleven years, and note the vacancy could be removed altogether by supposing Jeroboam reigned fifty-one or fifty-three years rather than forty-one — but they judge that unlikely, since the confusion of the numerals is improbable and the swift struggle for the throne, together with allusions in Hosea, rather favours anarchy breaking out immediately after Jeroboam's death.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the other road, holding that the thirty-eighth year "agrees with the assumption that Jeroboam reigned fifty-one years," so that no interregnum need be posited at all. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley simply notes that Zachariah reigned six months "after the throne had been vacant several years, through the dissensions that were in the kingdom." So the witnesses divide over the arithmetic — a long interregnum of twenty-two years, a shorter one of eleven, or none at all with a lengthened reign for Jeroboam — while agreeing on the substance Henry has drawn out: that Zachariah's brief reign is the first of a rapid, bloody succession in which the promise to Jehu is honoured to the letter even as the house of Jehu falls.
9:
The verdict on Zachariah is the dynasty's unchanging refrain — "he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, as his fathers had done: he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat" — and the witnesses read it as the sentence that seals the fate of Jehu's line. The reformed John Gill glosses "his fathers" as all his predecessors, from Jeroboam the son of Nebat onward, from whose sin in worshipping the calves they never departed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the phrase: "his fathers" means specifically the dynasty of Jehu, of which Zachariah was the last member; like all his predecessors he upheld the illicit worship established by the first Jeroboam.

Keil and Delitzsch draw out the theological force. Zachariah, too, persevered in the calf-worship of his fathers, and so the word of the LORD spoken over Jehu at 2 Kings 10:30 — that his sons to the fourth generation should sit on the throne — was fulfilled in him, and with him the promise expired. Then Shallum the son of Jabesh conspired against him and struck him down "before the people," openly, in the sight of all. Keil and Delitzsch set this murder in its larger frame: because Israel would not be brought to repentance either by the mercies shown under Joash and Jeroboam or by the severe judgments that went before, or by the earnest preaching of Hosea and Amos, the judgment of rejection could not fail at last to burst upon a nation that so basely despised the grace and covenant-faithfulness of God.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they make the verse a hinge: the fourth generation of Jehu sits on the throne exactly as promised, and precisely there the promise runs out, for the sin that ran through every reign of the dynasty has brought it to the edge of judgment. Zachariah's evil is not merely his own; it is the inherited, unbroken idolatry of a whole house, and his violent death — the first of the chapter's many — is the beginning of the rejection Keil and Delitzsch see hastening upon Israel with rapid steps. The formula that seems mere repetition carries, in their hands, the whole weight of a kingdom's doom: fidelity in God's promise to Jehu, and finality in God's judgment on Jehu's idolatrous sons. Gill's plain "the calves he departed not from" and Ellicott's "last member of the dynasty" together fix the point where blessing and curse meet in a single reign.
10:
Shallum's stroke opens the age of the assassins, and the witnesses attend both to the man and to the pointed phrase that he slew the king "before the people." The reformed John Gill notes that Shallum was, as Josephus calls him, a friend of Zachariah, emboldened by the people's dissatisfaction with their king. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads "before the people" as in some public assembly, the king having made himself odious, so that the murder was done in the open with the crowd's tacit consent.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes that openness the moral of the verse. "Son of Jabesh," he insists, is a patronymic — the father's name always being given in the case of usurpers — not "a man of Jabesh-Gilead." And "before the people" means in public: the open assassination of the king, in contrast with the secrecy that had cloaked former conspiracies, is a symptom of the rapidly increasing corruption of morals, a people so far gone that they could look on with indifference while their king was cut down. He records the puzzles of the Hebrew word qobol, which is Aramaic rather than Hebrew and occurs only here, transliterated by the Septuagint as a single strange word.

Here the witnesses touch a genuine oddity. Ellicott reports Ewald's acute conjecture that "Qobol-am," rather than meaning "before the people," was actually the proper name of yet another usurper — comparing the "third king during that month" of Zech. 11:8 — though Ellicott judges that on this reading the narrative would hardly be coherent; and he notes Grätz's alternative correction, "in Ibleam," a place-name. Keil and Delitzsch keep to the plainer sense, carrying the story forward: Shallum reigned but a single month before Menahem the son of Gadi came up from Tirzah, smote him, and took the throne in his stead.

The witnesses thus mostly converge — Shallum murdered his master openly and was murdered in turn within weeks — while preserving one real dissent, Ewald's reading of a hidden extra king in the corrupt Hebrew. What Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott agree on is the darkening moral atmosphere: conspiracy has become brazen, regicide a public spectacle, and the throne of Israel a thing seized and lost in a matter of days. The very awkwardness of the word "before the people" becomes, in Ellicott's hands, a witness to how far the nation had fallen — that such a deed could be done in daylight and provoke no horror.
11:
The closing-formula for Zachariah is the briefest kind of notice — "and the rest of the acts of Zachariah, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel" — and only one witness lingers over it. The reformed John Gill observes that the formula must cover not merely the six months of Zachariah's reign but "what he might do before in the interregnum," during the years the throne stood contested before he came to it.

The remark is small but telling. It reminds the reader that Zachariah was not an unknown who sprang up in a single half-year; he was the heir of Jeroboam II, a man who had waited, perhaps for years, through the dissensions that kept the throne vacant, before finally securing it for six brief months. Whatever he did in that long waiting — the maneuvering, the alliances, the struggle to hold his father's crown against rivals — is gathered, with his short reign, into the annals to which the historian defers.

The very existence of the formula is itself a quiet dignity. Even for a king who reigned but six months and died by an assassin's hand before the people, the standing phrase is used: his acts too are "written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel," as though the historian will grant the outward forms of a reign to a man the times allowed no time to reign at all. Gill's note keeps the reader from imagining Zachariah a mere cipher; there was a record of him, thin as it was, and it stretched back before his accession into the shadowed years of the interregnum.

Yet the reticence is eloquent too. The sacred writer says nothing of what those acts were, and by the standing formula levels the last of Jehu's line with all the rest — recorded, referred elsewhere, and passed by. Zachariah's whole public life, before and during his fragment of a reign, is folded into a single deferring sentence, and the narrative hurries on to the usurper who killed him. The formula honours the form of kingship even as the chapter strips away its substance, so that the fourth and final generation of Jehu's house is dismissed in a line, his one recorded certainty being the sword that ended him at v.10.
12:
The historian pauses to mark a prophecy fulfilled — "this was the word of the LORD which he spake unto Jehu, saying, Thy sons shall sit on the throne of Israel unto the fourth generation. And so it came to pass" — and the witnesses read in it the exactness of God's word, and its double edge. The reformed John Gill notes simply that the promise was now fulfilled in the short reign of Zachariah, the fourth of Jehu's line. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke counts the generations off: God had promised Jehu that his sons should sit on the throne to the fourth generation, and so they did — Jehoahaz, Joash, Jeroboam, and Zachariah succeeding him in turn.

But Clarke insists on the promise's limit and its reason. The word to Jehu was a reward for his zeal in destroying the house of Ahab and the worship of Baal; yet because Jehu executed that divine commission "with an uncommanded cruelty," God also cut his family short, according to the word by Hosea — "I will avenge the blood of Jezreel upon the house of Jehu, and I will cause to cease the kingdom of the house of Israel," at Hosea 1:4. So the very verse that celebrates a promise kept also marks a judgment fallen: the fourth generation both fulfils the blessing and exhausts it, and the blood Jehu shed at Jezreel comes home to his house.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a note on the verse's origin, reporting that Thenius considers this reflection to have been added by the Judean editor to the brief abstract of Zachariah's reign — a compiler's marginal theology written into the record. Whether the observation is the original historian's or a later editor's, the witnesses agree on its point. God's word to Jehu was fulfilled to the letter, neither a generation more nor less; the promise measured out four reigns and stopped, and the stopping was itself an act of justice for Jehu's excess. Gill's brevity, Clarke's careful pairing of reward and judgment, and Ellicott's textual note all serve one conviction: that the tangled, murderous politics of Israel run, without knowing it, along the exact lines God had drawn, so that even a usurper's knife falls precisely where prophecy had placed it.
13:
Shallum's reign is measured in a single phrase — "he reigned a full month in Samaria" — and the witnesses find in that fragment both a chronological nicety and a moral. The reformed John Gill notes only that the verse dates it to the thirty-ninth year of Uzziah, the same king called elsewhere Azariah, the two names used interchangeably through the chapter. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the lesson the brevity teaches: "that dominion seldom lasts long which is founded in blood and falsehood" — the throne Shallum seized by murder he held for barely thirty days.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills in how that month ended. Menahem, he explains, was one of Zachariah's generals; hearing of his master's murder while he lay with the troops at Tirzah, he hastened to Samaria, slew the usurper, and had himself proclaimed in his stead. When the people of Tiphsah would not open their gates to him, he took the place by assault and practised the most cruel barbarities, even ripping up the women that were with child — the first note of the savagery that will mark his reign.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the phrase "a full month," rendering it literally and recording a dissent. Thenius, he notes, argues that Shallum cannot have reigned a full month, since Zech. 11:8, with its three shepherds cut off in one month, is taken to refer to the three kings Zachariah, Shallum, and Menahem together, whose reigns would then have to be crowded into a single month. So a small debate opens over whether the "full month" is to be pressed literally or trimmed to fit the prophet's arithmetic.

The witnesses do not seriously divide. Gill fixes the date, Wesley the moral, Clarke the violent sequel, and Ellicott the one textual quibble; together they set before us a reign of thirty days bounded by two murders — Shallum killing to gain the throne and killed to lose it. The "full month" is just long enough to prove Wesley's maxim: a crown taken in blood is a crown quickly torn away, and the man who cut down Zachariah before the people is himself cut down by another general before the month is out. The rapid, self-consuming violence of the northern throne is compressed into this one verse's span.
14:
The instrument of Shallum's fall is named — "Menahem the son of Gadi went up from Tirzah, and came to Samaria, and smote Shallum... and slew him, and reigned in his stead" — and the witnesses fix both the geography and the man. The reformed John Gill locates Tirzah as a city in the tribe of Manasseh, the royal seat of the kings of Israel before Omri built Samaria, and, on Bunting's reckoning, some six miles from the capital; whether Menahem belonged to the city or was besieging it with an army under his command, Gill leaves open, though Josephus suggests the latter.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies Menahem as Zachariah's general, quartered with the troops at Tirzah, who on the news of his master's murder marched on the capital; the month of Shallum's reign was probably taken up with preparations for hostilities on both sides, until a battle at Samaria — or perhaps simply Menahem's entry with part of his forces — decided matters. Ellicott reads "son of Gadi" as possibly "a Gadite," and brings to bear the Assyrian record: Tiglath-Pileser notes in his annals that in his eighth regnal year, about 738 B.C., he took tribute of "Menihimmè Samerinâ'a" — Menahem the Samaritan — a rare and precious point where the sacred history and the cuneiform monuments touch the same king by name.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative on, noting that Menahem, having smitten Shallum, went on to smite Tiphsah and rip up its women with child, and reigned ten full years. The witnesses do not divide; between them they draw a portrait of the new master of Israel: a soldier who avenged his murdered king only to seize the throne himself, marching down from the old royal city of Tirzah to take Samaria, and confirmed to us not only by Scripture but by the boastful tribute-lists of Assyria. There is a grim continuity in it — the general who punishes one usurper becomes the next — and a sombre foreshadowing, for the same Assyrian king whose inscription names Menahem's tribute is the power to whom Menahem will soon hand over the wealth of Israel. The verse that begins his reign already, in Ellicott's cross-reference, points to the servitude that will define it.
15:
The closing notice on Shallum is unusually pointed, for it names not only his acts but "his conspiracy which he made" — and the single witness makes much of that addition. The reformed John Gill remarks that the book of the chronicles to which the reader is referred would no doubt have given an account of the cause of the conspiracy, and of the persons who assisted Shallum in it, along with whatever else he did in his short reign.

Gill's observation quietly underlines what is distinctive here. For most kings the formula records "the rest of the acts, and all that he did," or adds a note of their might; but Shallum's memorial is his treason. The one deed by which he is remembered, the deed the historian singles out for the annals, is the conspiracy that brought him to a throne he held for a month. Where other kings leave behind wars and buildings and reforms, Shallum leaves behind a plot — and the record of it, Gill supposes, would have named his fellow-conspirators, the men who backed his brief and bloody grasp at power.

There is a certain justice in the phrasing. Shallum's whole significance in Israel's history is that he murdered Zachariah "before the people" and reigned thirty days; and so the formula, which for a worthier king would gesture toward a lifetime of achievement, here gestures toward a single act of violence. The "rest of the acts" of a month-long reign can amount to little; the "conspiracy which he made" is the sum of the man. Gill's note lets the reader feel the shrinkage: a king reduced to the plot that made and unmade him, his name preserved in the chronicles chiefly as a warning of how the throne of Israel was now won and lost.

Yet the reticence is eloquent too. The sacred writer says nothing of what the conspiracy involved beyond that Shallum "made" it, and by the standing formula levels him with the kings before and after — recorded, referred elsewhere, and passed by. Even the standing phrase, bent to include his treason, becomes an epitaph on the anarchy of the age: a reign whose only recorded act was the crime that began it, and whose account is closed almost as soon as it is opened, the historian hurrying on to the tyrant Menahem who cut him down at v.14.
16:
The verse records an atrocity — "then Menahem smote Tiphsah, and all that were therein, and the coasts thereof from Tirzah... and all the women therein that were with child he ripped up" — and the witnesses divide first over where this Tiphsah lay. The reformed John Gill reports that the Jewish writers commonly take it for the well-known Tiphsah on the borders of Syria near the Euphrates, the Thapsacus that marked the far edge of Solomon's dominion; but he judges it "some place nearer Samaria, and Tirzah," and following Bunting sets it about six miles from the capital.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees and explains why. The name means "ford," and elsewhere denotes the famous Thapsacus on the Euphrates; but here an Israelite city in the neighbourhood of Tirzah is plainly intended, for the geography of the verse — "from Tirzah," meaning starting from Tirzah — shows the district of Tiphsah to have been conterminous with that of Menahem's base. He reconstructs the course of events: after slaying Shallum, Menahem returned to Tirzah and set out thence at the head of his whole army to bring the rest of the country to acknowledge him; when Tiphsah shut its gates and resisted his claim, he made an example of it savage enough to terrorise other towns into submission. Ellicott notes that Thenius would emend the name to Tappuah by the change of a single Hebrew letter, which suits the local indications well, though an otherwise unknown Tiphsah near Tirzah is not impossible.

On the horror itself the witnesses are restrained but clear. Ellicott parses "because they opened not to him" as an impersonal construction — the gates were closed against him — and refers the ripping up of the women with child to the like barbarities foretold of Hazael in 2 Kings 8:12 and denounced upon Ammon in Amos 1:13. There is no attempt to soften it. The new king secures his throne by a calculated terror against his own countrymen, butchering a city and its unborn for the crime of not opening its gates fast enough.

The witnesses thus contend mildly over the map — Thapsacus on the far Euphrates, a local Tiphsah near Tirzah, or Thenius's Tappuah — while agreeing wholly on the moral. Whatever the town's name and site, Menahem's reign is founded on an act of monstrous cruelty, and the historian records it without flinching as the true character of the man who now holds Samaria. The atrocity is not incidental; it is his policy, the terror by which a usurper without a claim compels a divided nation to bow.
17:
The regnal formula for Menahem seems at first a mere date — "in the nine and thirtieth year of Azariah king of Judah began Menahem the son of Gadi to reign over Israel, and reigned ten years in Samaria" — but the witnesses draw out the way it dovetails with the reigns just told. The reformed John Gill notes the neat consequence of Shallum's brevity: since Shallum reigned but a single month, his reign and Menahem's both fall in the same thirty-ninth year of Azariah, the one month swallowed up in the year that opens the other's decade.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a small precision to the round number: Menahem "reigned ten years — and some months over," the figure being given, as such figures often are, in completed years with the fraction left unspoken. It is a reminder that the tidy totals of the formula are approximations, the historian rounding to the nearest year even where a few months more must be reckoned to make the whole chronology of the chapter cohere.

Brief as their comments are, the two witnesses together let the verse do its structural work. After the whirl of the preceding reigns — a king of six months, a king of one — the formula plants a longer, steadier reign of ten years, the first in the chapter to give Israel any span of settled rule since Jeroboam II. Yet the stability is only relative and only apparent. The same verse that grants Menahem his decade has already told us, at v.16, how he began it — with the butchery of Tiphsah — and the verses that follow will show at what price he kept it, buying off the Assyrian with the silver wrung from his own people. Gill's dating and Ellicott's "some months over" are modest notes, but they anchor Menahem's ten years firmly in the chapter's tight chronological weave, where every reign is measured against the years of Judah's long-lived Uzziah, and where even a decade of rule turns out to be a decade of cruelty within and servitude without. The steadiest of these late reigns is steady only in its wickedness.
18:
The evaluation of Menahem repeats the refrain with one unusual addition — "he departed not all his days from the sins of Jeroboam" — and the witnesses fasten on both the phrase and the character it seals. The reformed John Gill notes only that the same character is given him as of those before: the calf-worship kept up, the sin of Jeroboam unforsaken. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott raises a mild dissent about the man's beginnings, reporting Ewald's view that at the outset Menahem appeared to be guided by better principles, on the strength of the shepherd-imagery of Zech. 11:4-8 — a promise of reform, if it was one, that his reign wholly belied.

Ellicott gives close attention to the odd words "all his days." They occur, he observes, at the very end of the verse in the Hebrew, and are found in no other instance of the common evaluation-formula the verse repeats; almost certainly they belong to the next verse, describing the whole span over which Pul's exaction weighed, rather than qualifying Menahem's sin. He notes too that the phrase "from upon the sins" is peculiar, and that the Septuagint's "from all the sins" appears to be the right reading. These are small textual points, but they show the witness weighing every word of a formula that lesser readers pass over as mere repetition.

Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to what the "all his days" introduces. In Menahem's time, they note, Pul king of Assyria invaded the land, and Menahem gave him a thousand talents of silver "that his hands might be with him, to confirm the kingdom in his hand." They report — and question — the interpretation, common since Ephraem Syrus and resting on Hosea 5:13, that Menahem himself invited Pul in order to establish his shaky rule with Assyrian help; the words of Hosea, "Ephraim goes to the Assyrian," they judge too general to be pinned so precisely on Menahem.

The witnesses converge on the verdict and diverge only on its edges. All agree that Menahem, whatever his beginnings, lived and died in the sin of Jeroboam; they differ over a displaced phrase, a variant reading, and whether the coming Assyrian was summoned or merely endured. What none of them softens is the substance: a usurper who secured his throne by butchery kept it by idolatry and, as the next verse will show, by selling his people's silver to a foreign empire.
19:
Assyria enters the story — "Pul the king of Assyria came against the land: and Menahem gave Pul a thousand talents of silver, that his hand might be with him to confirm the kingdom in his hand" — and the witnesses spend their energy on the identity of this Pul and the nature of the bargain. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks the moment's weight: this is the first mention of Assyria since its founder Nimrod in Gen. 10:11. He follows Prideaux in making Pul the father of the famous Sardanapalus, and glosses "that his hand might be with him" as meaning his power and influence, the word "hand" being frequently so used in Scripture.

The reformed John Gill wrestles with the man's place in history. Some, he reports, would identify Pul with Belochus or Belesis, the Babylonian governor who with Arbaces the Mede overthrew Sardanapalus and translated the empire to the Chaldeans; but Gill objects that Pul was no Babylonian but an Assyrian, indeed the first king of Assyria the Scriptures name, and cites the historian Metasthenes, who calls him Phulbelochus and gives him a reign of forty-eight years.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings the cuneiform evidence to bear and reaches a bolder conclusion. The verse begins abruptly, he notes, but the Septuagint restores the connection: "In his days Pul the king of Assyria." The name has been read on the monuments; and Ellicott lays out Schrader's elaborate argument, in ten linked steps, that Pul and Tiglath-Pileser are one and the same person — that Berosus calls Pul a Chaldean and Tiglath-Pileser calls himself king of Chaldea, that both bear the throne-name in the Babylonian records, and that no king named Pul appears in the Assyrian lists, so that Pul can only be identified with some other name there, and on historical grounds with Tiglath-Pileser alone. "Came against the land," he adds, means rather "came upon" and occupied it; the thousand talents amount to about three hundred and seventy-five thousand pounds; and the payment bought Pul's help to confirm Menahem as an Assyrian vassal against rival pretenders — for which Ellicott, unlike Keil, reads the invitation into the very phrase, comparing Hosea 5:13.

So the witnesses genuinely divide. Is Pul a distinct earlier Assyrian monarch — the first the Bible names, father of Sardanapalus, reigning forty-eight years — as Gill and Clarke suppose, or is he Tiglath-Pileser himself under an alternate name, as Ellicott and Schrader argue from the inscriptions? And was he summoned by a desperate usurper or simply an invader whom Menahem bought off? The witnesses do not resolve it; but they agree on the fact that matters most, that the throne of Israel is now propped up with Assyrian silver, and that the empire which will one day carry the nation away has, at Menahem's own invitation or with his own tribute, first set its hand upon the land.
20:
The verse shows how the tribute was raised — "Menahem exacted the money of Israel, even of all the mighty men of wealth, of each man fifty shekels of silver, to give to the king of Assyria" — and the witnesses turn accountant. The reformed John Gill notes the policy in the levy: Menahem exacted it of the men most able to pay, "the mighty men of wealth," and so spared and perhaps conciliated the poor, laying the burden where it could be borne. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reckons the individual assessment at upwards of five pounds sterling a man.

Keil and Delitzsch and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpen the philology and the sum. "Exacted," Ellicott explains, is literally "caused to go out," a word used of laying out or expending money, though he suspects the true reading may be "imposed"; and the "mighty men of wealth," a phrase that in older Hebrew meant the heroes of the host, has here shrunk to a later sense — men of property, not warriors, as Keil and Delitzsch also insist. Then Ellicott does the arithmetic that lays bare the scale of the exaction: the talent was worth three thousand shekels, so a thousand talents came to three million shekels; at fifty shekels a head — one maneh, the Assyrian mana and the Greek mina — the payment implies some sixty thousand men of property in the kingdom able to contribute.

Ellicott draws out what that arithmetic betrays about the state of the north. There was no great Temple treasury in Israel to draw upon, as there was at Jerusalem, and any palace hoards had disappeared in the confusions of the frequent revolutions; so the whole crushing sum had to be squeezed directly out of the pockets of the propertied classes. Keil and Delitzsch set the moment in its larger significance: Pul was the first king of Assyria to invade the kingdom of Israel, and by this invasion he prepared the way for the conquest of the kingdom by his successors and for the extension of Assyrian power as far as Egypt.

The witnesses do not divide; between them they let a single tax return tell a nation's story. Sixty thousand wealthy men, each stripped of fifty shekels, to buy off an emperor who has merely to appear to be paid — and behind the transaction, the ominous fact Keil and Delitzsch name, that this is the first Assyrian footfall on Israelite soil, the opening of the door through which the whole kingdom will one day be carried out. Gill's note on the sparing of the poor and Clarke's tidy five pounds a head are the small change of the passage; its true weight is in Ellicott's sixty thousand and Keil's "first" — a people mortgaging itself, class by class, to the power that will destroy it.
21:
The formula for Menahem is the standard deferral — "the rest of the acts of Menahem, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel?" — and the witnesses treat it partly as a literary convention and partly as a genuine dead end. The reformed John Gill notes that the reader is referred to the same book of chronicles as for the rest of the kings, "which seems to be a form the historian uses of them all," a standing phrase rather than a special commendation.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke presses the point further and more bleakly: "there are no chronicles extant in which there is anything farther relative to this king." The record to which the historian points has perished; whatever more Menahem did beyond the butchery of Tiphsah and the buying-off of Pul is lost to us, so that the formula gestures toward a book we no longer possess. For a reign of ten years, that is a striking poverty of memory.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative past the formula to the succession it introduces. Menahem, unlike most of the chapter's kings, "slept with his fathers," dying a natural death, and Pekahiah his son reigned in his stead — a rare dynastic continuity in an age of assassins. Yet the peace was thin: Pekahiah, they note, reigned in the spirit of his predecessors, and only for two years, before his own aide-de-camp Pekah conspired against him and cut him down.

The witnesses converge on a quiet irony. Menahem, the cruelest of these kings, is one of the few to die in his bed and pass his crown to his son; the very savagery and Assyrian silver that secured his throne bought his line a brief hereditary succession the milder usurpers never won. But the formula that closes his acts points to a vanished book, and the son to whom he passed the crown will hold it only two years before the pattern of murder resumes. Gill's "a form he uses of them all" and Clarke's "no chronicles extant" together mark how little of Menahem endures: a standing phrase, a lost record, and a short-lived heir who will fall, as v.25 will tell, to one of his own officers.
22:
The death-notice is a single line — "and Menahem slept with his fathers; and Pekahiah his son reigned in his stead" — and the one witness who lingers over it catches the surprising thing about it. The reformed John Gill observes that Menahem "died a natural death, and in peace, though an usurper and a tyrant" — the very man who had waded to the throne through Shallum's blood and made his name with the butchery of Tiphsah is granted the quiet end that the chapter denies to almost all his fellows.

Gill's terse contrast carries a whole meditation. The formula "slept with his fathers" is the language of peace, of a king laid to rest among his ancestors and succeeded by his son; and it is used here of one of the most brutal figures in the book. There is no poetic justice in the manner of his death. The tyrant who ripped up the women of a resisting city dies in his bed; the usurper who bought off an empire with his people's silver passes the crown, unbroken, to his own son. The reader who expected the sword to find Menahem as it found Zachariah and Shallum is left to reckon with a providence that does not always repay blood with blood within a single reign.

Yet the peace is only Menahem's own, and only for a moment. The same verse that gives him his quiet grave names the son who inherits his throne and his guilt, and the next verses will show that son cut down within two years by one of his own officers. Menahem escapes the reckoning; his house does not.

Gill's insistence that the tyrant died "in peace" is not a softening but a sharpening: it forces the reader to see that God's judgments are not always immediate, that a wicked man may prosper to the end of his days and hand on his ill-gotten crown, and that the account is nonetheless not closed but only deferred to the next generation. The brief, peaceable formula, set over so violent a life, becomes a study in the patience — and the postponement — of divine justice, a reminder that a quiet death is no acquittal, and that the blood a man escapes in his own person may yet be required of his line.
23:
Pekahiah's accession is dated with the chapter's usual precision — "in the fiftieth year of Azariah king of Judah, Pekahiah the son of Menahem began to reign over Israel in Samaria, and reigned two years" — and the single witness here is occupied with a small gap in the count. The reformed John Gill notes the arithmetic: since Menahem began to reign in the thirty-ninth year of Azariah and reigned ten years, those years should have ended in the forty-ninth of Azariah, yet the son does not begin until the fiftieth. There must therefore be "an interregnum of a year."

Gill offers a plausible reason for the vacancy: "perhaps the title of Pekahiah might be disputed, and it was a year before he could get settled on the throne." Even in a rare instance of hereditary succession, the crown did not pass without challenge; the son of Menahem, for all his father's ruthless consolidation of power, had to make good his claim before he could reign, and a year slipped by in the contesting of it. The very smoothness the dynasty seemed to have won at the previous verse proves, on closer reckoning, to have cost a year of uncertainty.

The observation is modest, but it fits the chapter's larger picture and the historian's exact chronology. Gill's care to account for a single missing year reflects the same concern that runs through the whole chapter — to make the reigns of Israel and Judah dovetail against the fixed measure of Uzziah's fifty-two years — and it quietly confirms how unstable the northern throne had become. A dynasty that had killed to gain the crown could not simply bequeath it; between Menahem's death and Pekahiah's reign lies a year of disputed title, a small blank in the record that Gill's reckoning fills.

And the reign it introduces will be the shortest kind of respite, for the two years here allotted to Pekahiah will end, as the next verses tell, under the sword of one of his own captains. The lone witness, attending only to a year's discrepancy, lets the reader see how precarious even an inherited crown had grown in the last days of the kingdom — a throne so contested that a son of a strong king needed a year to secure it and held it only two more before he lost it and his life together.
24:
The verdict on Pekahiah is the familiar one — "he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat" — and the witnesses read it as one more entry in the dynasty's unbroken ledger. The reformed John Gill notes that, besides his other sins, Pekahiah "cleaved to that of worshipping the calves, a piece of state policy all the kings of Israel gave into" — the phrase capturing exactly how the idolatry had become less a matter of conviction than of statecraft, an inherited policy of the throne that no northern king would abandon for fear of loosening his hold on the people.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch pass quickly over the formula to the violent end it precedes. Pekah the son of Remaliah, one of Pekahiah's own captains, conspired against him and smote him in Samaria, in the citadel of the king's house, "with Argob and Arieh, and with him fifty men of the Gileadites," and killed him and reigned in his room. Keil and Delitzsch also flag, in passing, the notorious chronological knot attached to the usurper: Pekah is credited with a reign of twenty years, a figure hard to reconcile with the later notices that place his death in the fourth year of Ahaz, a difficulty they will take up more fully when his reign is reckoned.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they show the brief experiment in hereditary succession collapsing back into the chapter's grim pattern. Menahem had died in his bed and left his throne to his son; but the son, keeping up the same calf-worship and the same evil, lasts only two years before a trusted officer, backed by fifty Gileadite swords, murders him in the very stronghold of his palace. Gill's "a piece of state policy" and Keil's account of the palace assassination meet in a single verdict: the sin that the kings of Israel clung to for the sake of their thrones did nothing to secure those thrones, and Pekahiah, faithful to the calves to the end, is cut down among them. The idolatry that was meant to bind the kingdom to its kings binds it instead to its ruin, one murdered king after another, until there is no dynasty left to murder.
25:
The manner of Pekah's coup is told with a few dark, obscure details, and the witnesses probe both his rank and his mysterious companions. The reformed John Gill takes up the Hebrew title given him — "a captain of his" — explaining that the word signifies a "third man," reckoned by some the third in rank to the king, while Josephus makes him a chiliarch, a commander of a thousand. Either way he was a trusted officer close to the throne, which made his treachery the more deadly: the king was struck down by one of the very men set to guard him.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke confronts the puzzle of the two names that accompany the deed — Pekah smote Pekahiah "with Argob and Arieh." Who were they? Clarke honestly reports the divided opinion: "some make them men, some make them statues." On the one reading Argob and Arieh were fellow-conspirators cut down with the king, or perhaps chief courtiers slain beside him; on the other they were not persons at all but objects — statues, or fixtures of the palace — near which the murder was done. Clarke does not resolve it, and adds only the firmer detail that Pekah had fifty Gileadites in the conspiracy with him.

The witnesses leave the obscurity standing, and it is worth leaving. The verse comes to us from the chronicles of a chaotic age, and its very murkiness — a captain who is a "third man" or a chiliarch, two names that may be henchmen or may be masonry, fifty Gileadite swords in a palace stronghold — conveys something of the confusion of the event itself. What is clear is the shape of the deed: a high officer of the king, with a band of provincial soldiers from Gilead behind him, forces his way into the citadel of the palace and murders his master to seize the crown.

Gill's philology and Clarke's candid "some say men, some say statues" together preserve both the certainty and the mystery of the scene: the certainty that Pekah killed his king by treachery from within, and the mystery of the strange companions whose names the historian records but does not explain. In an age when the throne changed hands by the knife, even the record of a murder comes down half in shadow, and the reader is left, like the witnesses, to weigh names the sacred writer set down but never expounded.
26:
Once more the historian closes a reign with the standing formula — "and the rest of the acts of Pekahiah, and all that he did, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel" — and the single witness notes only its sameness. The reformed John Gill remarks that "the same form of expression is used as before, of all the kings," the phrase recurring unchanged from reign to reign.

Gill's brevity is itself a kind of commentary. For a king who reigned but two years and was murdered in his own palace, there is little to say, and the historian says little; the formula is deployed as it is for every king, great or small, faithful or wicked, long-lived or cut off in weeks. That very uniformity levels Pekahiah with the rest — his short and evil reign gathered up, like all the others, into a single deferring sentence that points to a book of chronicles now lost. The reader is told that a record existed and is left to imagine its contents, for the sacred writer preserves none of them.

Yet the repetition is not empty. By using the same closing phrase for Pekahiah as for kings who reigned decades and shaped nations, the historian quietly makes a point about the age: these northern reigns have become so brief, so violent, and so barren of anything worth remembering that the standing formula is almost all that can be said of them. Pekahiah kept up the calves, reigned two years, and died by treachery; his "rest of the acts" can have amounted to little, and even that little is consigned to a vanished chronicle.

Gill's plain observation that the form is the same as for all the others captures the pathos of it — a king reduced to a formula, his whole reign a line of boilerplate between the murder that ended his father's dynasty and the murder that began his own. The very sameness of the words measures how interchangeable, and how expendable, the last kings of Israel had become: their names change, the formula does not, and the reader who has met the same sentence a dozen times feels, in its very monotony, the exhaustion of a kingdom running out of both kings and memory.
27:
Pekah's reign is dated to the very close of Judah's long reign — "in the two and fiftieth year of Azariah king of Judah Pekah the son of Remaliah began to reign over Israel in Samaria, and reigned twenty years" — and the single witness marks the coincidence of the years. The reformed John Gill notes that this fifty-second year "was the last year of the reign of Azariah," so that Pekah's accession falls exactly as Uzziah's long life ends and Jotham's sole reign begins, the two kingdoms' clocks meeting at this point.

The observation, brief as it is, anchors one of the chapter's key chronological pivots. Uzziah's fifty-two years have been the fixed measure against which every northern reign has been dated — the six-month reign of Zachariah in his thirty-eighth year, Shallum's month in his thirty-ninth, Menahem's decade from the same year, Pekahiah's two years from his fiftieth — and now, in his fifty-second and final year, the throne of Israel passes to Pekah, who will hold it, the verse says, for twenty years. Gill lets the reader feel the hinge: as Judah's great and stable reign closes, Israel's most durable of these late kings takes power.

Yet the twenty years assigned to Pekah are among the most disputed figures in the book, for later notices place his death in the fourth year of Ahaz, which will not easily accommodate so long a reign — a difficulty the witnesses take up at the verses that follow. Gill himself does not wrestle with it here; he is content to fix the starting point, tying Pekah's first year to Uzziah's last.

The effect is to bind the fortunes of the two kingdoms tightly together at the moment of transition. Just as the pious, leprous Uzziah is laid to rest and the godly Jotham steps forward in Judah, the murderer Pekah — who has just cut down his master in the palace — mounts the throne of Israel for a reign that will bring the Assyrian storm down upon both nations. Gill's single, spare note on "the last year of Azariah" quietly sets that ominous parallel before the reader: two kingdoms turning a corner together, one toward a brief reprieve under a good king, the other toward the beginning of the end under a usurper whose long reign will end in the first great deportation.
28:
The evaluation of Pekah adds nothing new to the formula — "he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat" — but the witnesses use it as the occasion to weigh his whole guilty reign and the disaster it invited. The reformed John Gill spreads the charge across the man's crimes: not only the usurpation and the murder by which he seized the throne, but idolatry, and particularly the worship of the calves that the formula points to. Pekah's evil is thus threefold — treason, blood, and false worship — and the historian's brief verdict gathers all three under the old indictment of walking in Jeroboam's sin.

Keil and Delitzsch look past the formula to the catastrophe that fell in Pekah's days and to the tangled chronology of his end. In his reign came Tiglath-Pileser king of Assyria and stripped away Ijon, Abel-beth-maachah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor, Gilead, and Galilee, all the land of Naphtali, carrying the people captive; and Pekah himself at last met death in a conspiracy by Hoshea the son of Elah, who made himself king, the verse says, "in the twentieth year of Jotham." That datum, Keil and Delitzsch note, is very strange, since Jotham reigned only sixteen years, and Ahaz began in the seventeenth year of Pekah, so that Pekah's death would fall in the fourth year of Ahaz; the reason for so odd a reckoning they trace, following Usher, to the way the years were counted.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill measures the depth of Pekah's sin, and Keil and Delitzsch measure the depth of the judgment it drew down — for it is in this reign, under this evil king, that the Assyrian first tears whole provinces from Israel and leads their people into exile. The formula that seems mere repetition marks, in fact, the turning point of the northern kingdom's ruin: the same Pekah who kept up the calves loses Gilead and Galilee and Naphtali to Tiglath-Pileser, and the chapter's long tale of idolatry and murder passes here into the beginning of the great captivity. Gill's catalogue of his crimes and Keil's account of the dismembered land together frame the verdict: an evil that is the nation's own, and a judgment that is the empire's sword, meeting in the reign of the last strong king of a doomed people.
29:
The first great deportation is recorded here — "in the days of Pekah king of Israel came Tiglath-Pileser king of Assyria, and took Ijon, and Abel-beth-maachah, and Janoah, and Kedesh, and Hazor, and Gilead, and Galilee, all the land of Naphtali, and carried them captive to Assyria" — and the witnesses attend both to the conqueror and to the lost territory. The reformed John Gill dwells on the name Tiglath-Pileser, reporting the forms a Jewish chronologer and Metasthenes give it — Pul-Asir, Phul-Assar, with a reign of twenty-five years — and the guess that he was the son of the Pul of v.19. He even parses the name into its supposed elements: Diglath, from the river Tigris that washed Assyria, joined to Pul or Bel, the god Baal or Jupiter, and Azar, taken for Mars.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the king in the succession of empires, making him, on Prideaux's reckoning, the successor of Sardanapalus, the same figure Aelian calls Thilgamus and Usher names Ninus junior, who with Belesis headed the conspiracy that overthrew Sardanapalus and fixed his seat at Nineveh. But Clarke's chief concern is the geography of the loss. These cities, he explains, had a long and contested history: they belonged to Israel, were taken by Ben-hadad of Syria when he was in league with Asa of Judah, as 1 Kings 15:20 records; they were regained by Jeroboam II; and now they are torn from Israel once more, this time for good. From 1 Chronicles 5:26 Clarke adds that Pul and Tiglath-Pileser together carried away the two and a half trans-Jordan tribes — Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh — and these, he notes soberly, "were never restored to Israel."

The witnesses do not divide; between them they mark the verse as the hinge of the kingdom's doom. The northern cities and the whole eastern territory, fought over for generations between Israel and Syria and briefly recovered under Jeroboam II, now pass into Assyrian hands and their inhabitants into an exile from which they never return. Gill's fascination with the conqueror's name and Clarke's tracing of the cities' long history serve one end: to show that the judgment so often threatened in this chapter has now begun in earnest. The first tribes go into captivity under Pekah, the strong king; the calf-worship that promised to keep the kingdom whole has ended in its dismemberment; and the "never restored" of Clarke's note falls like a knell over Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh, the first of Israel to vanish into the Assyrian night.
30:
The chain of assassinations reaches its last link within the chapter — "and Hoshea the son of Elah made a conspiracy against Pekah the son of Remaliah, and smote him, and slew him, and reigned in his stead, in the twentieth year of Jotham the son of Uzziah" — and the witnesses read it both as poetic justice and as a chronological riddle. The reformed John Gill catches the symmetry: Hoshea "did by him as he had done by Pekahiah," slaying the slayer, so that, as the Jews say, it was measure for measure — the captain who murdered his king struck down in turn by another conspirator.

But the date "in the twentieth year of Jotham" is a puzzle, since Jotham reigned only sixteen years, and the witnesses labour to reconcile it. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the phrase to mean not the twentieth year of Jotham's reign but the twentieth year "after the beginning of Jotham's reign" — which is the same as the fourth year of Ahaz, Jotham's son, the counting continued from Jotham's accession though Jotham himself was dead. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets out the fuller reconciliation he draws from Calmet, distinguishing the stages of Hoshea's rise: Hoshea conspired against Pekah in the twentieth year of Pekah, which was the eighteenth after Jotham began to reign; two years later, in the fourth year of Ahaz — the "twentieth of Jotham" of this verse — he made himself master of part of the kingdom; and only in the twelfth year of Ahaz did he come into peaceable possession of the whole, as 2 Kings 17:1 records.

The witnesses converge on the solution while dividing over its details. All agree that "the twentieth year of Jotham" cannot mean the twentieth year of a sixteen-year reign, and all resolve it by continuing the count from Jotham's accession into the reign of his son — whether, with Wesley, as a simple equivalence to Ahaz's fourth year, or, with Clarke and Calmet, as one stage in a drawn-out seizure of power that was not complete until years later. Gill's "measure for measure" gives the moral, and the Wesleyan witnesses give the arithmetic; together they show the throne of Israel changing hands one final time within the chapter, by the same treachery that has marked every reign, even as the historian's dating quietly reaches forward past Jotham's death into the years of Ahaz, under whom the last act of the tragedy will be played out.
31:
The closing-formula for Pekah is the usual one — "and the rest of the acts of Pekah, and all that he did, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel" — and the witnesses give it brief attention before turning to the chronology of the transition. The reformed John Gill notes only that Pekah's acts not recorded here "were to be read in the book of chronicles of the kings so often referred to," the standing deferral once more.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the seam between the reigns to check the chapter's arithmetic. The next verse dates Jotham's accession in Judah to "the second year of Pekah," and this, they observe, agrees with the statement at v.27 that Pekah became king in the last year of Uzziah, supposing that accession fell at the very beginning of the year. From this it follows that Jotham's sixteen years came to a close in the seventeenth year of Pekah's reign, the point at which, according to 2 Kings 16:1, Ahaz his son began to reign.

The witnesses do not divide; they simply hand the narrative from Israel back to Judah. With Pekah's acts consigned to the chronicles, the historian returns to the southern kingdom to give the reign of Jotham, whose accession he now synchronises with Pekah's second year. Gill's plain note on the formula and Keil's careful dovetailing of the years together perform the transition the chapter's structure requires: the long, murderous account of the northern kings is punctuated here, as it was at the start, by the steadier record of Judah.

The formula closes the file on Pekah — though he still lives and reigns, his death not yet fallen until the earlier notice at v.30 anticipated it — and clears the way for the brief, better reign of Jotham that the chapter will set, by way of contrast, against all the blood that has gone before. The seam is quiet, but Keil's arithmetic makes it exact, and the reader is carried without a jolt from the anarchy of Samaria to the ordered succession of Jerusalem, where a good king reigns while the northern kingdom slides toward its end.
32:
The narrative turns from Israel's carnage to Judah's calm — "in the second year of Pekah the son of Remaliah king of Israel began Jotham the son of Uzziah king of Judah to reign" — and the witnesses use the accession to introduce a rare good reign and to fix its place in the chronology. The reformed John Gill works out the synchronism: Pekah began in the fifty-second year of Uzziah, which was that king's last, and so Pekah's first; Uzziah reigned the full fifty-two years and then Jotham succeeded, which brings Jotham's accession to the beginning of Pekah's second year.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, reaching the end of this crowded chapter, gathers the whole reign of Jotham into a single warm portrait. Jotham reigned well, doing that which was right in the sight of the LORD; Henry cites Josephus's high character of him — pious toward God, just toward men, laid out for the public good, wanting no virtue that became a good prince. Though he did not take away the high places, he showed great respect to the temple and built its higher gate; and Henry draws the magistrate's lesson, that if rulers cannot pull down the high places of sin, let them at least build and beautify the high gate of God's house. Yet Jotham "died in the midst of his days," at forty-one — and Henry pauses on a striking observation, that Scripture records the ages of the kings of Judah but not of the kings of Israel, an honour God put upon the house of David, and that none of Judah's kings reached David's seventy, Jotham dying younger than most. His early death Henry reads as a judgment on an unworthy people, and a mercy to himself, for it spared him the sight of his degenerate son.

Henry finds the shadow of that son already falling. In Jotham's days, he notes, the confederacy of Rezin king of Syria and Pekah the son of Remaliah began to form against Judah — the alliance that would so terrify Ahaz that his heart and his people's were moved "as the trees of the wood are moved with the wind," in the words of Isa. 7:2. Though the confederates were unjust, the historian will say at v.37 that "the LORD began to send them against Judah"; for men, Henry says, are God's hand, the sword and the rod he uses as he pleases to serve his righteous counsels, even when their own intentions are wicked. The storm gathered in the reign of pious Jotham, but he came to his grave in peace, and it broke instead upon his faithless heir.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill fixes the date and Henry paints the man, and between them they set the reign of Jotham as a brief southern light against the northern darkness — a good king, honoured by God with a recorded age and a peaceful death, who beautified the temple he could not fully purify, and in whose quiet reign the clouds of a coming judgment first began to gather over the house of David.
33:
The regnal formula for Jotham is given in full — "five and twenty years old was he when he began to reign, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Jerusha, the daughter of Zadok" — and the witnesses read it against the years of regency that went before. The reformed John Gill enters one clarifying word: Jotham began to reign "alone, after the death of his father," the sixteen years here counted being his sole reign, distinct from the earlier years when, as v.5 told, he had already governed as viceroy while the leprous Uzziah lived shut away.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the formula as the frame of a reign like his father's in its main lines — right in the eyes of the LORD, yet with the high places still standing — and note the one act the verse's context records, that "he built the higher gate of the house of the LORD." But their eye is chiefly on the ominous note struck at the reign's close. It is evident from the position of the remark at v.37, they observe, that the incursions of the allied Syrians and Israelites under Rezin and Pekah began already in the closing years of Jotham, so that the good king's last days were shadowed by the first raids of the confederacy that would fall in force upon his son.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill's small correction — that these sixteen years are Jotham's own, after the regency — and Keil's larger observation together give the reign its true shape: a well-prepared king, seasoned by years of governing in his father's name, who reigned rightly for sixteen years and built up the house of God, but whose reign ended under the gathering storm of the Syro-Ephraimite alliance.

The mother's name, Jerusha the daughter of Zadok, is preserved as always for the kings of Judah, a mark of the recorded lineage of David's house that the northern kings were never granted; not one of the murderers and usurpers across the Jordan is given a mother's name or a stated age, while the son of David is set down with both. The bare formula thus carries, in the witnesses' hands, both the dignity of an ordered succession and the first shadow of the trouble the next verses will name — a good reign, exactly dated and honourably descended, drawing to its close just as the enemies of Judah begin to move.
34:
The evaluation of Jotham is unusually unqualified — "he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD: he did according to all that his father Uzziah had done" — and the single witness draws out how high that commendation reaches. The reformed John Gill reports a striking rabbinic observation from Jarchi: in all the kings of Judah before Jotham, even the best of them, some sin was found; but in Jotham "there was nothing scandalous and reproachful." He was, on this reading, the one king of the line against whom the record enters no particular blot.

Gill reinforces the point with the high character Josephus gives him — that there was no virtue wanting in him: he was pious toward God, just toward men, and careful of the public good. Yet Gill is careful to define the ground of the biblical commendation. The inspired historian, he notes, "chiefly respects matters of religious worship"; the "right" that Jotham did is above all that "he did not give into idolatry." The verdict is a verdict on his fidelity to the LORD's worship, and it is precisely there that Jotham stood clear, keeping himself and his throne from the calf-worship of the north and the foreign cults that would corrupt his son.

The lone witness thus lets the verse mark a high point in a dark chapter. Set among the murderers and idolaters of Israel, and even measured against the flawed good kings of his own line, Jotham stands out as a ruler in whom Scripture finds nothing to reproach in the matter that most concerns it — the worship of God.
