Gill's pairing of Jarchi's "nothing scandalous" with Josephus's catalogue of virtues and his own insistence that the historian's chief concern is worship gives the reader a rounded sense of the man: pious, just, publicly useful, and above all untainted by idolatry. The one shadow the verse leaves — that, like his father, he did not remove the high places — the next verse will name; but the commendation itself is as full as the book grants to any king, and the witness lets it stand at its full height before the qualification comes. In a chapter crowded with evil, the plain "he did that which was right," entered without the usual reservation in the verse itself, shines the brighter for the darkness around it.
35:
The one reservation on Jotham's good reign is the familiar one, paired with the one building he raised — "howbeit the high places were not removed: the people sacrificed and burned incense still in the high places. He built the higher gate of the house of the LORD." The reformed John Gill notes that the high places were not removed by Jotham "as they were not by his father, and the same is observed of the best of kings before" — the chronic exception that qualified even the most faithful reigns of Judah, from Asa and Jehoshaphat down to Uzziah and now his son.

The witnesses take the failure as no peculiar fault of Jotham but the standing limit of the pre-Hezekian reformation. Good kings suppressed idolatry and honoured the temple, yet left untouched the local shrines where the people, even in the name of the LORD, worshipped outside the appointed sanctuary. Jotham is no worse than his fathers in this; he is simply one more in the long line who did right in all else and yet let the high places stand, so that the people's irregular worship went on beneath an orthodox throne.

On the higher gate the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds a point of location. The gate Jotham built, he explains, was "not of the temple, but of one of the courts of the temple" — probably the gate that led toward the king's palace, connecting the royal house to the courts of the LORD. It was a work of adornment and access rather than of reformation: Jotham could not, or would not, pull down the high places of the land, but he could beautify and dignify the house of God, strengthening the link between the throne and the temple.

The two witnesses do not divide; between them they give the verse its balanced sense. Jotham's reign is marked by the same "howbeit" that qualifies every good king before Hezekiah — the high places left standing, the people's worship uncorrected — and by a single positive act, the building of the higher gate. Gill sets the failure in its long context, and Wesley locates the gate; together they show a pious king doing what such kings characteristically did, advancing the honour of the temple while leaving the deeper reform for a later hand. The building and the "howbeit" stand side by side, the exact measure of a reign that was genuinely good and yet, like all before it, not yet thorough.
36:
The closing-formula for Jotham points, as usual, to the fuller record — "now the rest of the acts of Jotham, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?" — and here the witnesses can actually supply much of what it defers. The reformed John Gill notes that some of Jotham's other acts are recorded in the canonical book of Chronicles, directing the reader there for what the historian of Kings passes over.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out that fuller account from 2 Chronicles 27. Jotham, he relates, built the high gate of the house of the LORD and much on the wall of Ophel; he built cities in the mountains of Judah and, in the forests, castles and towers; he overthrew the Ammonites and obliged them to pay him, for three years running, a hundred talents of silver and ten thousand measures each of wheat and barley. He reigned sixteen years, having begun at twenty-five — a vigorous, constructive reign of fortification, colonisation, and successful war, of which the historian here records only the barest formula.

On the higher gate Clarke enters a note of learned uncertainty. Commentators are not agreed, he observes, what exactly it was: some think it a gate Jotham then made which had not existed before, and thus the same as the "new gate" mentioned in Jer. 26:10 — a plausible identification, he judges, though not a certain one. The point is small, but it shows the witness weighing how the terse notice of v.35 connects to the later history of the temple's structures.

The witnesses do not divide; between them they fill out the deferred record. Gill points to Chronicles, and Clarke unfolds what Chronicles contains — a reign far busier and more successful than the formula suggests, its gates and walls and towers and tribute all gathered, in the book of Kings, into a single sentence of reference. The effect is once more that of proportion: the sacred writer of Kings keeps his own account lean, recording of Jotham chiefly his fidelity and his one temple-gate, and trusting the chronicles for the rest. Clarke's catalogue of buildings and victories lets the reader glimpse the substance behind the formula — a strong and pious king whose works filled the annals, summed up here in the modest question, "are they not written?"
37:
The reign of Jotham closes on a note of gathering menace — "in those days the LORD began to send against Judah Rezin the king of Syria, and Pekah the son of Remaliah" — and the witnesses weigh both the timing of the threat and the sobering theology of who sends it. The reformed John Gill fixes the "in those days" carefully: it means at the end of Jotham's days, or even after his death, the confederacy being in design and preparation while he lived but only breaking out in force in the times of his son Ahaz. The storm was forming in the good king's reign; it fell in the bad king's.

What arrests the witnesses is the verb: it was the LORD who "began to send" these enemies against Judah. Though Rezin and Pekah were unjust aggressors, invading a kingdom that had given them no just cause, the historian traces their assault to the hand of God, who uses even wicked men as the sword and the rod of his righteous purposes, so that their unrighteous intentions serve his righteous counsels. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke widens the lens to the whole Assyrian calamity that this moment opens: it was about this time, he observes, that the Assyrian wars so ruinous to the Jews began, though they reached their highest pitch of disaster in the reigns that followed.

Clarke cannot record the coming affliction without a pastor's compassion and a prophet's hope. However much we may blame the Jews for their disobedience and obstinacy, he writes, we cannot help feeling for them under their severe afflictions: grievously they have sinned, and grievously have they suffered for it. And if they are still objects of God's judgments, there is revelation to believe they will yet be objects of his goodness — though he adds the honest caution that there is as yet no evidence among the people themselves that the day of their redemption is at hand, for "they do not humble themselves; they do not seek the Lord."

The witnesses do not divide. Gill fixes the timing, and Clarke draws out both the historical scale and the moral, but both rest on the verse's own startling claim — that the LORD "began to send" the enemy. The affliction of Judah is not mere politics; it is the beginning of a divine chastisement, launched in the last days of a pious king and destined to crush his faithless successors. Gill's "in design before, but came to pass in his son's times" and Clarke's sweep from these first raids to the long ruin of the nation together make the verse a threshold: the peace of Jotham ending, and the judgment of God, using Syria and Israel as his instruments, beginning to move against the house of David.
38:
The chapter ends as it began, with a death and a succession — "and Jotham slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David his father: and Ahaz his son reigned in his stead." The reformed John Gill glosses the formula plainly: Jotham "died, and was buried with the kings of Judah in their sepulchres," laid to rest with the honour of David's line in the royal tombs of the city. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, for their part, simply carry the verse forward, joining Jotham's burial to the accession of Ahaz and the opening of the next reign.

The witnesses add little, and the restraint is fitting, for the weight of the verse lies in the name it ends on. Jotham is buried peaceably among his fathers in the city of David, the reward of a good and quiet reign; but the son who reigns in his stead is Ahaz, under whom, as the previous verses have warned, the confederacy of Rezin and Pekah will break in fury upon Judah and the long Assyrian ruin will gather to its height. The formula gives Jotham the dignity of rest with his fathers; it hands the throne to the king who will undo much of what his father and grandfather built.

So the chapter that has told of a leper-king humbled for his pride, of five northern kings murdered in swift succession, of the first Assyrian tribute and the first Assyrian deportation, and of one brief good reign in Judah, closes on the threshold of worse to come. Jotham "slept with his fathers" in peace — the last of the chapter's many deaths, and almost the only one not by the sword — and Ahaz took his place.

Gill's quiet notice of the royal burial and Keil's bare recording of the succession together let the chapter end on a hinge: a good king laid honourably to rest, and a faithless son stepping into a reign already darkened by the enemies his father's last days had seen begin to move. The peace of Jotham's grave is real, but it is the calm before the storm the next chapter will unleash, and the witnesses, saying almost nothing, let the single name "Ahaz" carry all the foreboding the verse requires.
